issues.original: This article in the series covers most of the production wins. The other models are interesting, but the LLMs are what every product I've shipped on Bailian calls every minute of every day. The official Qwen API reference is dense and complete; this article is the readable companion that guides you through it.
issues.fixed: This article in the series covers most of the production wins. While the other models are interesting, the LLMs are what every product I've shipped on Bailian calls every minute of every day. The official Qwen API reference is dense and complete; this article is the readable companion that guides you through it.

issues.original: The Qwen family is large. Most teams overspend by defaulting to [CODE] everywhere. Most teams underspend on quality by defaulting to [CODE]. The right answer is 'match variant to job':
issues.fixed: The Qwen family is large. Most teams overspend by defaulting to [CODE] everywhere and underspend on quality by defaulting to [CODE]. The right answer is 'match variant to job':

issues.original: Whether you use the OpenAI compat layer or DashScope native, the core of a chat-completion request remains the same: a model ID, a messages array, and a parameter block.
issues.fixed: Whether you use the OpenAI compat layer or DashScope native, the core of a chat-completion request remains the same: a model ID, a messages array, and a parameter block.

issues.original: A common mistake is using [CODE] for embedding-style classification. Don't. Use [CODE] with a tight system prompt and you'll cut cost 10× with no quality loss on tasks where you only need a label.
issues.fixed: A common mistake is using [CODE] for embedding-style classification. Instead, use [CODE] with a tight system prompt. This will cut costs by 10× without losing quality on tasks that only need a label.

issues.original: The Qwen function-calling protocol is the OpenAI tool-calls protocol. Two LLM calls plus your code in the middle:
issues.fixed: The Qwen function-calling protocol follows the OpenAI tool-calls protocol. It involves two LLM calls and your code in between:

issues.original: For structured output, don't rely on prompting. Use:
issues.fixed: For structured output, don't rely on prompting. Use:

issues.original: Qwen3 series models support **[CODE]** — it asks the model to produce a reasoning chain before the final answer. Quality goes up, especially on reasoning-heavy tasks. **But you must use streaming.** Non-stream returns a 400.
issues.fixed: Qwen3 series models support **[CODE]**, which prompts the model to produce a reasoning chain before the final answer. Quality improves, especially on reasoning-heavy tasks. **But you must use streaming.** Non-streaming returns a 400 error.

issues.original: I forward [CODE] to my logging system and never to the user. Three reasons: (1) it leaks chain-of-thought IP if customers ever see it, (2) it confuses non-technical readers, (3) it doubles your visible response length.
issues.fixed: I forward [CODE] to my logging system and never to the user for three reasons: (1) it leaks chain-of-thought IP if customers see it, (2) it confuses non-technical readers, and (3) it doubles the visible response length.

issues.original: The single biggest surprise for teams coming from the OpenAI ecosystem: **[CODE] lies about Qwen token counts**. The Qwen tokenizer is not BPE-compatible with [CODE] or [CODE]. If you size your context budget with [CODE], you'll be off by 20-40% on Chinese, 5-10% on English. I lost a Friday night to a RAG pipeline that was 'definitely under the 32k context' by tiktoken count and was actually 41k by Qwen's count.
issues.fixed: The single biggest surprise for teams coming from the OpenAI ecosystem: **[CODE] lies about Qwen token counts**. The Qwen tokenizer is not BPE-compatible with [CODE] or [CODE]. If you size your context budget with [CODE], you'll be off by 20-40% on Chinese and 5-10% on English. I lost a Friday night to a RAG pipeline that was 'definitely under the 32k context' by tiktoken count but was actually 41k by Qwen's count.

issues.original: Of all the Bailian models, Qwen-Omni has saved me from the most product-roadmap issues. "Can you tell me what's happening in this 2-minute promo video?" used to take 3 weeks, involving frame extraction, captioning each frame, and stitching them together. With Qwen-Omni, it's just one HTTP request. However, the documentation lacks details on some pitfalls, like the fact that streaming is mandatory, which has cost more than one team a half-day. Let's avoid that for you.
issues.fixed: Of all the Bailian models, Qwen-Omni has saved me the most from product-roadmap issues. "Can you tell me what's happening in this 2-minute promo video?" used to take 3 weeks, involving frame extraction, captioning each frame, and stitching them together. With Qwen-Omni, it's just one HTTP request. However, the documentation lacks details on some pitfalls, such as the requirement for streaming, which has cost more than one team a half-day. Let's avoid that for you.

issues.original: The docs note streaming as a feature, but they bury the fact that **for Qwen-Omni it is required**. Set [CODE] and you get a 400 with a message about the model requiring streaming.
issues.fixed: The docs mention streaming as a feature, but they bury the fact that **Qwen-Omni requires it**. Set [CODE], and you get a 400 error with a message about the model requiring streaming.

issues.original: If your downstream code expects a complete string, buffer the deltas yourself: It is one extra function. You will write it once.
issues.fixed: If your downstream code expects a complete string, buffer the deltas yourself. It's just one extra function you'll write once.

issues.original: The cost knob the docs are quiet about: [CODE] and [CODE] for images, and the equivalent fps / resize parameters for video. By default Qwen-Omni will process video at native resolution and a default fps. For a 2-minute 1080p clip that is a *lot* of token-equivalents, and the bill scales with it.
issues.fixed: The cost settings the docs don't highlight: [CODE] and [CODE] for images, and the equivalent fps / resize parameters for video. By default, Qwen-Omni processes video at native resolution and a default fps. For a 2-minute 1080p clip, that's a *lot* of token-equivalents, and the bill scales with it.

issues.original: What I do in production: - **Images for understanding tasks** — [CODE]. Almost no quality loss for "what's in this image" tasks, big cost savings. Set [CODE] so the model never scales up tiny crops. - **Video for description tasks** — pre-resize to 720p before upload, downsample fps to 4 for static-ish content (people talking) or 8 for action content (sports, fast cuts). Above 8 fps you are usually paying for redundant frames. - **Long video** — chunk it. The model has a context limit. For anything over ~3 minutes, split into 90-second chunks, summarize each, then summarize-the-summaries with [CODE]. Same pattern as long-document RAG.
issues.fixed: In production, I do the following: - **Images for understanding tasks** — [CODE]. There's almost no quality loss for "what's in this image" tasks, and it saves a lot of costs. Set [CODE] to prevent the model from scaling up tiny crops. - **Video for description tasks** — pre-resize to 720p before uploading, and downsample fps to 4 for static content (like people talking) or 8 for action content (like sports or fast cuts). Above 8 fps, you're usually paying for redundant frames. - **Long video** — chunk it. The model has a context limit. For anything over ~3 minutes, split into 90-second chunks, summarize each, and then summarize the summaries with [CODE]. This follows the same pattern as long-document RAG.

issues.original: You have two options. The docs cover both. **Path 1 (preferred): upload to OSS, send signed URL.** Then pass [CODE] as the [CODE] field. This is the right answer for anything bigger than 30 seconds because base64 inflates the payload by 33%. **Path 2: base64 inline.** Useful for short clips, avoids the round trip to OSS.
issues.fixed: You have two options, both covered in the docs. **Path 1 (preferred): Upload to OSS and send a signed URL.** Pass [CODE] as the [CODE] field. This is the best choice for anything over 30 seconds because base64 encoding inflates the payload by 33%. **Path 2: Inline base64.** This is useful for short clips and avoids the round trip to OSS.

issues.original: For pure transcription, Bailian also exposes a dedicated **Paraformer** ASR model that is cheaper. Use Paraformer for transcribe-only and Qwen-Omni when you need understanding (sentiment, summarization, "did the call mention pricing").
issues.fixed: For pure transcription, Bailian also offers a dedicated **Paraformer** ASR model, which is cheaper. Use Paraformer for transcription only and Qwen-Omni when you need understanding (sentiment, summarization, "did the call mention pricing").

issues.original: The recurring pattern I ship for AI marketing: a creative team uploads a 60-second product video; we want a structured caption ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]). One Qwen-Omni call, JSON mode (yes it works with multimodal), under 4 seconds wall clock for 720p input.
issues.fixed: The recurring pattern I use for AI marketing: a creative team uploads a 60-second product video, and we want a structured caption ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]). One Qwen-Omni call in JSON mode (which works with multimodal) takes under 4 seconds for 720p input.

issues.original: The Qwen-Omni audio path looks simple — pass an [CODE] part with [CODE] and [CODE] — but the encoding requirements are strict in ways that don't surface as friendly errors. Get them wrong and you get a 400 with "unsupported audio format" and no further guidance.
issues.fixed: The Qwen-Omni audio path seems simple — pass an [CODE] part with [CODE] and [CODE] — but the encoding requirements are strict and don't always result in friendly errors. If you get them wrong, you'll receive a 400 error with "unsupported audio format" and no further guidance.

issues.original: Wanxiang is the API that has done the most for our marketing pipeline and caused the most production surprises. The model is genuinely good — [CODE] produces 720p clips that pass for an actual video team's output most of the time — but the surface around it is async, native-protocol, has expiring URLs, and rate-limits in non-obvious ways.
issues.fixed: Wanxiang is the API that has done the most for our marketing pipeline and caused the most production surprises. The model is genuinely good — [CODE] produces 720p clips that often pass for an actual video team's output — but the surrounding features are async, use a native protocol, have expiring URLs, and impose rate limits in non-obvious ways.

issues.original: This article is the version of the docs that has been through six months of 'why is this happening at 2am' tickets.
issues.fixed: This article is the version of the docs refined over six months of 'why is this happening at 2am' tickets.

issues.original: Polling every second is wasteful and gets you rate-limited. Polling every 30 seconds wastes user time. The backoff schedule I use:
issues.fixed: Polling every second is wasteful and leads to rate limiting. Polling every 30 seconds wastes user time. Here’s the backoff schedule I use:

issues.original: Start at 5 seconds, multiply by 1.45 each iteration, cap at 60 seconds. A typical 720p 5-second clip finishes in 30-90 seconds, so the median user waits about 4 polls.
issues.fixed: Start at 5 seconds, multiply by 1.45 each iteration, and cap at 60 seconds. A typical 720p 5-second clip finishes in 30-90 seconds, so the median user waits about 4 polls.

issues.original: The single most expensive mistake I have seen in production: someone fetched the [CODE], displayed it on the site, and then the page broke 24 hours later when the URL stopped resolving. The URLs Wanxiang returns are signed and time-bound. **Always copy the file to your own OSS bucket on success:**

issues.fixed: A surprisingly large part of Wanxiang's quality depends on the prompt. After a few months of iteration, this structure works well:

issues.original: Things that hurt quality: - Negative prompts in the main prompt ('no text on screen'). Use a [CODE] parameter if you need them. - More than ~3 main subjects. The model conflates them. - Specific brand or person names. Generic descriptions work better. - Anything cyrillic / Arabic / Devanagari script as text-on-frame. Wanxiang is currently English- and Chinese-text aware; other scripts come out as garbled glyphs.
issues.fixed: Things that hurt quality: - Negative prompts in the main prompt ('no text on screen'). Use a [CODE] parameter if needed. - More than ~3 main subjects. The model conflates them. - Specific brand or person names. Generic descriptions work better. - Text in Cyrillic, Arabic, or Devanagari scripts. Wanxiang is currently English- and Chinese-text aware; other scripts appear as garbled glyphs.

issues.original: Generate three variants per critical prompt with different seeds ([CODE] parameter). One of them is usually the right one.
issues.fixed: Generate three variants per critical prompt with different seeds ([CODE] parameter). One of them is usually correct.

issues.original: Wanxiang bills per second of video. A 5-second 720p clip costs a few RMB. Concurrent task limits are per API key. For production traffic, request a quota increase via the console before you launch. The default of 5 concurrent tasks per workspace is fine for prototyping but insufficient for a real product.
issues.fixed: Wanxiang bills per second of video. A 5-second 720p clip costs a few RMB. Concurrent task limits are per API key. For production traffic, request a quota increase via the console before launching. The default of 5 concurrent tasks per workspace is fine for prototyping but insufficient for a real product.

issues.original: The polling-with-backoff approach in the previous section is the simplest pattern that works. It's sufficient for a single user-initiated request. For a production marketing pipeline that submits 200 videos a day, polling burns API calls, and engineering simplicity competes with cost. The alternatives:
issues.fixed: The polling-with-backoff approach in the previous section is the simplest and works for a single user-initiated request. For a production marketing pipeline that submits 200 videos a day, polling consumes too many API calls, and engineering simplicity competes with cost. The alternatives are:

issues.original: Three things webhooks force you to handle: - **Public endpoint required.** DashScope can't POST into your VPC. Either expose via a public load balancer or use a relay (I run an Nginx in front that auth-checks and forwards into the private network). - **Idempotency.** Webhooks can fire twice. Always check whether you've already archived this [CODE] before doing it again. - **Failure mode is silent.** If your webhook endpoint is down when DashScope tries to deliver, you don't get a retry. Always pair the webhook with a 'scan tasks older than 10 minutes that aren't terminal' cleanup job.
issues.fixed: Three things webhooks require: - **Public endpoint.** DashScope can't POST into your VPC. Expose it via a public load balancer or use a relay (I run an Nginx in front that auth-checks and forwards to the private network). - **Idempotency.** Webhooks can fire twice. Always check if you've already archived this [CODE] before doing it again. - **Silent failure.** If your webhook endpoint is down when DashScope tries to deliver, you won't get a retry. Pair the webhook with a 'scan tasks older than 10 minutes that aren't terminal' cleanup job.

issues.original: Every Chinese-language product I've worked on uses Qwen-TTS-Flash, not because it's the cheapest TTS API, but because it's the only one that handles **mainland Chinese dialects** (Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu) and English in the same SDK, with voices that don't sound like a 2019 customs announcement. After six months of using it for a marketing-video voice-over pipeline, this is what I wish someone had told me on day one.
issues.fixed: Every Chinese-language product I've worked on uses Qwen-TTS-Flash, not because it's the cheapest TTS API, but because it's the only one that handles **mainland Chinese dialects** (Cantonese, Sichuanese, Wu) and English in the same SDK, with voices that don't sound like a 2019 customs announcement. After six months of using it for a marketing video voice-over pipeline, this is what I wish someone had told me on day one.

issues.original: According to the model card, Qwen-TTS-Flash offers 40+ voices. The ones I use most: For Mandarin product narration my default is [CODE] (warm, 30-something female) for marketing content and [CODE] (steady, 40-something male) for tutorial / explainer content. For Cantonese ad spots [CODE] is the safe choice. The voice names are stable but new voices are added regularly — fetch the canonical list from the model card before you pin one in production code.
issues.fixed: According to the model card, Qwen-TTS-Flash offers 40+ voices. The ones I use most: For Mandarin product narration, my default is [CODE] (warm, 30-something female) for marketing content and [CODE] (steady, 40-something male) for tutorial/explainer content. For Cantonese ad spots, [CODE] is the safe choice. The voice names are stable, but new voices are added regularly—fetch the canonical list from the model card before pinning one in production code.

issues.original: Qwen-TTS doesn't use the OpenAI compat layer. You call it via the DashScope native SDK: A minimal request: Two key points: - The output is a *URL* by default, not bytes. Like Wanxiang, **download it within 24 hours** (I do it immediately and re-upload to my own OSS bucket). - [CODE] defaults to [CODE]. WAV is also available; for downstream concatenation work I prefer WAV because there's no MP3 header overhead per chunk.
issues.fixed: Qwen-TTS doesn't use the OpenAI compat layer. You call it via the DashScope native SDK. A minimal request: Two key points: - The output is a *URL* by default, not bytes. Like Wanxiang, **download it within 24 hours** (I do it immediately and re-upload to my own OSS bucket). - [CODE] defaults to [CODE]. WAV is also available; for downstream concatenation, I prefer WAV because there's no MP3 header overhead per chunk.

issues.original: For voice-bot use cases (real-time conversational UIs), you want streaming. The deltas are audio bytes you can write directly to a player or a file: Time-to-first-byte for streaming is typically under 400ms in the Shanghai region, which users perceive as immediate. Non-streaming for a 30-second utterance takes around 4-6 seconds, which is fine for batch narration but sluggish for chat.
issues.fixed: For voice-bot use cases (real-time conversational UIs), you want streaming. The deltas are audio bytes you can write directly to a player or a file. Time-to-first-byte for streaming is typically under 400ms in the Shanghai region, which users perceive as immediate. Non-streaming for a 30-second utterance takes around 4-6 seconds, which is fine for batch narration but sluggish for chat.

issues.original: Qwen-TTS does language *detection* from the text, but if you mix scripts you should set [CODE] explicitly. My production rules: - Pure Mandarin text → [CODE] (default). - Pure English text → [CODE]. Voices like [CODE] shine here. - Cantonese text in Traditional Chinese → [CODE], voice [CODE] or [CODE]. - Mixed CJK + English (common in tech narration): leave the language unset; the model handles code-switching surprisingly well.
issues.fixed: Qwen-TTS detects language from the text, but if you mix scripts, set [CODE] explicitly. My production rules: - Pure Mandarin text → [CODE] (default). - Pure English text → [CODE]. Voices like [CODE] shine here. - Cantonese text in Traditional Chinese → [CODE], voice [CODE] or [CODE]. - Mixed CJK + English (common in tech narration): leave the language unset; the model handles code-switching surprisingly well.

issues.original: The docs list SSML support but don't specify which tags work. From experience: - [CODE] — works. Use for pauses between sentences in marketing copy. - [CODE] — works. - [CODE] — works. [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], or numeric percentage. - [CODE] — works for relative changes (e.g. [CODE]). - [CODE] — works for phone numbers, codes, dates. - [CODE] — partial. Tone-marked pinyin is more reliable than IPA on Chinese. - [CODE] — does NOT work. You cannot mid-utterance switch voice. Use separate calls and concatenate.

Tone-marked pinyin is more reliable than IPA on Chinese. - [CODE] — does not work. You cannot switch voices mid-utterance. Use separate calls and concatenate.

issues.original: Why split? Two reasons: (1) per-call latency is much lower for short utterances, so synthesizing in parallel is faster; (2) you can patch a single bad sentence by re-rolling just that one without redoing the whole take. We use this for ad spots — the [CODE] tags handle the inter-sentence pauses, and the parallel synth means a 60-second clip is ready in ~4 seconds.
issues.fixed: Why split? Two reasons: (1) per-call latency is much lower for short utterances, so synthesizing in parallel is faster; (2) you can patch a single bad sentence by re-rolling just that one without redoing the whole take. We use this for ad spots—the [CODE] tags handle the inter-sentence pauses, and the parallel synthesis means a 60-second clip is ready in ~4 seconds.

issues.original: Billing is per second of output audio. Streaming and non-streaming costs are the same. A 60-second ad spot costs a few RMB—much cheaper than a voice actor's hourly rate and fast enough for the marketing team to iterate dozens of variations in an afternoon.
issues.fixed: Billing is per second of output audio. Streaming and non-streaming costs are the same. A 60-second ad spot costs a few RMB—much cheaper than a voice actor's hourly rate and fast enough for the marketing team to iterate through dozens of variations in an afternoon.

issues.original: A real example from my marketing pipeline: The [CODE] makes 'DSL' come out as letter-by-letter (correct for an acronym). The [CODE] adds a deliberate pause where listeners need to absorb. The [CODE] slows the URL recommendation slightly so listeners can write it down. None of this is possible with plain text input.
